{"key_event":"Thousand Days’ War and Reyes centralization","reasoning":"The civil war, hyperinflation, insecurity, Panama’s secession, and Reyes’s closure of Congress weakened autonomous parties, regional elites, and representative checks, though coffee regions and local producer networks preserved some associational capacity. State power suffered severe wartime and territorial shocks, but by 1908 executive centralization, fiscal stabilization, army professionalization, departmental fragmentation, and public works restored a moderate central capacity in the remaining territory.","society_change":-0.8,"society_power":4.3,"state_change":0.2,"state_power":5.6}
